text: Abstract: This paper examines the social processes involved in Tengger ethnicity preservation and attempts to explain the complex dynamics of cultural preservation in the face of Islamization and Indonesian state intervention. Research was carried out in the hamlets of Ngadisari and Sapikerep, in the district of Probolinggo. By employing representation theory, this study investigates how Tengger ethnic individuals negotiate and project their identity in the context of the modern nation state and its institutional dynamics. Based on the dialectics of Tengger tradition and Islamization/modern state intervention, two important findings arise. Firstly, the cultural system of the Tengger has a robust nature that can accommodate Hinduism and Islam as forms of symbolic identity rather than real conversion to these religion systems. Secondly, Islamization and Hinduization in the Tengger region are apparent and their social culture can be seen as synergetic. Such syncretism is also reflected in the "Javanese Islam" concept of a syncretic exterior that hides a substantial Sufi-based Islam.
